meta_description: Learn how to calculate every component of your CLT termination: vacation, 13th salary, FGTS, 40% penalty, notice period. Complete guide with comparison tables.

hero.title: How to Calculate Labor Termination (CLT)
hero.subtitle: The definitive guide to verifying that your employer is paying every cent you are entitled to upon dismissal.

related_tools.title: Labor Termination Calculator
related_tools.description: Simulate your complete termination package with all values detailed.

content.text: Ignorance of your labor rights is the most expensive tax you pay.
content.author: Adapted popular proverb

content.text: Did you know that 7 out of 10 workers don't check their termination values and simply sign the document? Worse: it is estimated that about 30% of terminations contain errors that harm the worker. These errors range from incorrect proportional vacation calculations to the omission of habitual overtime effects. This guide exists so that you never become one of these statistics.

Labor termination is the most critical financial moment of the employment relationship. It's when all accumulated rights during the contract are converted into amounts that, combined, can represent several months' salary. Making errors in this calculation can mean losing thousands of reais.
Before calculating, it is essential to understand how you were dismissed, as each modality generates completely different rights. See the complete comparison table:

content.expression: Proportional Vacation = (Salary ÷ 12) × Months in the Accrual Period

content.text: For every 12 months of work, you acquire the right to 30 days of vacation. If dismissed before completing a period, you receive proportionally. On top of this amount, the constitutional 1/3 bonus is applied. Attention: if you have accrued vacation (complete accrual period without leave), it must be paid double according to Article 137 of the CLT.
Notice Period Proportional to Length of Service

content.expression: Notice Period = 30 days + (3 days × complete years worked), maximum 120 days

content.text: In dismissal without just cause, the company must compensate the notice period if it doesn't require the employee to work during it. Each complete year of service adds 3 days. See the progression table:

A crucial detail: the indemnified notice period counts as length of service. This means it can cause you to complete another month for purposes of proportional vacation and proportional 13th salary. For example, if you were dismissed after 11 months and 15 days, the 30 days of notice period complete 12 months and 15 days — guaranteeing full vacation.

The employer deposits monthly 8% of your gross salary (including habitual overtime, night shift bonus, and other salary benefits) into an FGTS account at Caixa Econômica Federal. Upon dismissal without just cause, you withdraw the entire accumulated balance during the contract plus a 40% penalty on that balance.

This penalty is the largest financial slice of the termination and exists to protect the worker who was involuntarily dismissed. For a worker with a R$ 3,500 salary and 5 years at the company, the accumulated FGTS would be approximately R$ 3,500 × 0.08 × 60 months = R$ 16,800, and the 40% penalty would be R$ 6,720.

The company has a deadline of 10 calendar days after the end of the contract to pay all termination benefits (Art. 477, §6° CLT). Failure to meet this deadline generates a penalty equal to one month's salary of the worker, according to §8° of the same article. If the company is late, you are entitled to this additional penalty.
Checklist: What to Verify in Your Termination

Before signing the Termination Certificate (TRCT), verify: (1) that the salary balance is correct (days worked in the last month); (2) that habitual overtime was included in the calculation base for 13th and vacation; (3) that the proportional notice period has the correct year count; (4) that accrued vacation is doubled if past the deadline; (5) that the company delivered the forms for FGTS withdrawal and unemployment insurance.

If you find errors, do not sign the TRCT without registering your disagreement in writing. Contact your union or a labor lawyer. You have up to 2 years after dismissal to file a labor lawsuit, and can claim amounts from the last 5 years of the contract.
